metadata.summary: Configure Azure NAT Gateway for managed outbound connectivity from private subnets: static egress IPs, zone redundancy, SNAT port sizing, and monitoring for port exhaustion.

content.title: Azure NAT Gateway for Outbound Connectivity
content.summary: NAT Gateway provides managed, zone-redundant outbound internet connectivity for private subnets. It replaces the implicit Load Balancer outbound rules with a predictable static public IP, consistent SNAT behavior, and configurable idle timeout. Every public IP on a NAT Gateway provides ~64,000 SNAT ports — size the IP count against expected concurrent outbound connections.

why.p: Private subnets (no public IP resources) need outbound internet access for: pulling container images, calling external APIs, OS package updates, sending telemetry. Without NAT Gateway or an alternative egress path, Azure routes outbound traffic through a shared, unpredictable SNAT pool — the IP changes without notice and breaks third-party IP allowlists.

Load Balancer outbound rules are the older alternative, but they are per-NIC and SNAT port counts are shared with inbound connections — port exhaustion is silent and causes dropped outbound connections that look like random timeouts to the application. NAT Gateway is the recommended replacement.

NAT Gateway has a hard limit of ~64,000 SNAT ports per public IP. High-fanout workloads (web scrapers, batch HTTP, connection poolers that open many short-lived connections) silently drop connections at the edge when this is exhausted. Monitor `SNATConnectionCount` metric.

list.item: Any private subnet that requires outbound internet access (container registries, OS updates, external APIs).
Workloads that must present a consistent egress IP for third-party IP allowlisting (payment gateways, SaaS APIs).
High-availability setups requiring zone-redundant outbound — NAT Gateway supports zone-redundant public IPs.
AKS node pools in private subnets — nodes need outbound access for node bootstrap and image pull.
Subnets that have no outbound internet requirement — NAT Gateway costs even when idle; skip it for data-tier or internal-only subnets.
Virtual WAN spoke VNets — Virtual WAN has its own managed egress; adding a NAT Gateway causes routing conflicts.
Subnets with Azure Firewall as the egress path — do not attach NAT Gateway and Firewall UDR to the same subnet; only one egress path should control outbound routing.

rule.statement: Size NAT Gateway public IP count based on expected peak outbound concurrent connections: each public IP provides ~64,000 SNAT ports. Add one additional public IP per 50,000 expected concurrent outbound connections. For web scrapers, batch HTTP, or connection-heavy workloads exceeding 50,000 concurrent connections, use multiple public IPs (max 16 per NAT Gateway) or a public IP prefix.
rule.rationale: SNAT port exhaustion silently drops connections — the application sees random timeouts with no Azure error. The default single-IP configuration is sufficient for typical workloads (<10,000 concurrent outbound) but fails for high-fanout patterns.

rule.statement: Use Standard SKU public IP with zone-redundant allocation (zones: ["1", "2", "3"]) for production NAT Gateways. Basic SKU public IPs are not supported with NAT Gateway.
rule.rationale: Zone-redundant IP ensures NAT Gateway survives an AZ failure. Basic SKU IPs are incompatible with NAT Gateway and cause a deploy-time error that is confusing to diagnose.

example.title: NAT Gateway with zone-redundant IP (Terraform)
NAT Gateway with Basic SKU IP
example.note: NAT Gateway requires Standard SKU and Static allocation. Basic SKU causes a deploy-time error: "NAT Gateway is not supported with Basic SKU public IP."

rule.statement: Set `idle_timeout_in_minutes` based on application connection patterns. Default is 4 minutes; increase to 10-15 minutes for applications that keep long-lived TCP connections idle (database connection pools, websockets, gRPC streams). Do NOT set it to 120 minutes (maximum) by default — connections remain in the SNAT table longer, consuming ports.
rule.rationale: SNAT port exhaustion is exacerbated by long idle timeouts. Each idle connection holds a SNAT port for the duration of the timeout. Connection pools that recycle connections frequently benefit from shorter timeouts (4-10 min); only set higher for genuinely long-lived streams.

rule.statement: Configure metric alerts for `SNATConnectionCount` dropping below expected thresholds and for SNAT port utilization approaching 80% of capacity. Enable diagnostic logs on the NAT Gateway to a Log Analytics workspace. Alert on connection drops (`DroppedPackets` metric) as an early warning of SNAT exhaustion.
rule.rationale: SNAT exhaustion manifests as silent connection drops — no Azure error, no application error code, just timeouts. Without proactive monitoring, high-fanout workloads fail intermittently in production before the issue is identified.

checklist.check: Standard SKU public IP with Static allocation.
IP count sized for expected concurrent outbound connections (64k ports per IP).
Idle timeout set to match application connection patterns (default 4 min, max 120 min).
NAT Gateway associated with private subnets requiring outbound access.
Outbound IP documented for third-party allowlisting.
NAT Gateway NOT attached to subnets with Azure Firewall UDR.

antipattern.description: NAT Gateway has SNAT port exhaustion (~64k per public IP); high-fanout outbound workloads (web scrapers, batch HTTP) silently drop connections at the edge.
antipattern.reason: Agents configure a single public IP for all workloads. Add one IP per 50,000 expected concurrent connections; use a public IP prefix for large-scale egress.

antipattern.description: Agents attach NAT Gateway to a subnet that also has a UDR pointing to Azure Firewall. Both compete for the default route; behavior is undefined.
antipattern.reason: Only one egress mechanism should control the default route (0.0.0.0/0) for a subnet. Use either NAT Gateway or Azure Firewall + UDR — not both.
